{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|12=draft}} (present-day northern {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft}}). As a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸文|12=draft}}, its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hagoromo Legend|ja|羽衣伝説|12=draft}}. These excerpts feature waka written in [[man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the {{ill|Amanohashidate|ja|天橋立|12=draft}} (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=draft}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=draft}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ({{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|12=draft}}), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=draft}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=draft}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=draft}}, in modern-day {{ill|Kyotango City|ja|京丹後市|12=draft}}). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=draft}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|12=draft}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=draft}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=draft}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by {{ill|Takeda Sukeyoshi|ja|武田祐吉|12=draft}}, Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).

An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=draft}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|12=draft}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=draft}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=draft}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by {{ill|Takeda Sukeyoshi|ja|武田祐吉|12=draft}}, Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣|12=draft}} - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|12=draft|12=draft}} (present-day northern {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|12=draft|12=draft}}). As a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸文|12=draft|12=draft}}, its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hagoromo Legend|ja|羽衣伝説|12=draft|12=draft}}. These excerpts feature waka written in [[man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the {{ill|Amanohashidate|ja|天橋立|12=draft|12=draft}} (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=draft|12=draft}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=draft|12=draft}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ({{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|12=draft|12=draft}}), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=draft|12=draft}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=draft|12=draft}}, in modern-day {{ill|Kyotango City|ja|京丹後市|12=draft|12=draft}}). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=draft|12=draft}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|12=draft|12=draft}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=draft|12=draft}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by {{ill|Takeda Sukeyoshi|ja|武田祐吉|12=draft|12=draft}}, Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣|12=draft|12=draft}} - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: 47 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:丹後国風土記 up to rev 103401231
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2008年10月}} '''『丹後国風土記』'''（たんごのくにふどき）は、[[丹後国]]（現在の[[京都府]][[京都府北部地域|北部]]）の[[風土記]]。[[逸文|逸書]]であるため、内容は『[[釈日本紀|釋日本紀]]』などでの引用によるしかない。風土記編纂が命じられたのが[[和銅]]6年（[[713年]]）であるため、原本は遅くとも[[8世紀]]中にはできていたと思われる。 数多くある風土記逸文の中でも比較的長文が残されており、最古の部類に入る[[浦島伝説]]、[[羽衣伝説]]の記述は[[万葉仮名]]書きの[[和歌]]が入っている点も含めて特筆すべきものである。他に、[[天橋立]]の伝承もある（下に挙げる和歌は[[仮名交じり文|漢字仮名交じり文]]で記す）。 また、これとは別に『丹後国風土記残缺』が風土記の写本の一部として伝えられているが、これについては後世の偽書である可能性が高い福岡猛志「『丹後国風土記残欠』の基礎的検討」『愛知県史研究』17号、2013年。 == 浦島伝説 == {{画像提供依頼|浦嶋子伝説関連史跡|date=2019年7月|cat=京都府}} 「筒川嶼子　水江浦島子」という項目に記述がある。「天上仙家」や「蓬山」が出てくるなど中国渡来の[[神仙思想]]が窺える。「水江浦島子」が[[童話]]に出てくる[[浦島太郎]]であるが、彼は[[日下部氏|日下部首]]の一人である。 : 筒川の里、日下部首等の先祖に姿容秀美の筒川嶼子という者、即ち水江浦島子がいた。伊豫部馬養連の記したところのものを述べる。 と前置きした上で、長谷朝倉宮御宇天皇（[[雄略天皇]]）の御世、浦島子は小舟に乗り釣りに出た。三日三晩の間一匹の魚も釣れなかったが五色の亀だけ得る。奇異に思ったが眠っている間に亀は比べることもなき美麗な婦人と為った。女娘は問答の中「天上仙家之人也」と己を語る。彼女が眠るように命じ浦島子が目覚めると、不意の間に海中の大きな島に至っていた。館の門に入ると七人の童子、八人の童子が迎えるが彼らはそれぞれ「すばるぼし」（[[プレアデス星団|プレアデス]]）と「あめふりぼし」（[[ヒアデス星団|ヒヤデス]]）だという。女娘は父母と共に迎え、歓待の合間に人界と仙都の別を説く。館に留まること三年経ち、浦島子は郷里の事を思い出し、神仙之堺に居るよりも俗世に還ることを希望する。女娘は別れを悲しみながらも、玉匣（たまくしげ）を渡し「戻ってくる気ならゆめゆめ開けるなかれ」と忠告する。帰り着いて辺りが変わっているので郷の者に聞くと、浦島子は蒼海に出たまま帰らなかったということにされていた。玉匣を開くと風雲に翩飛けるような変化が起き、浦島子は涙に咽（むせ）び徘徊し、歌を詠む…… ::常世邊に　雲立ち渡る　水江の　浦嶋の子が　言持ち渡る : 神女遙飛,芳音で歌いて曰く： ::倭邊に　風吹き上げて　雲離れ　退き居り共よ　我を忘らすな : 浦嶼子： ::子等に戀ひ　朝戸を開き　我が居れば　常世の濱の　波の音聞こゆ : 後世の人歌いて曰く： ::水江の　浦嶋の子が　玉匣　開けず有りせば　復も會はましを ::常世邊に　雲立ち渡る　多由女　雲は繼がめど　我そ悲しき 別の書『[[古事談]]』では、「[[淳和天皇]]御宇天長二年（825年）乙巳。丹後国与佐郡人水江浦島子。此年乗松船。到故郷」と記され、そのことから帰還まで350年程度経ったと推定される。出発時の雄略天皇の代がいつなのか確定しがたいが、他の浦島伝説での共通点も踏まえ現世では館での3年より遙かに長い時間が流れていたと伝えられることは確実なようである。 == 羽衣伝説 == {{also|奈具神社#由緒}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|磯砂山の登山口付近の「羽衣茶屋」]] {{画像提供依頼|羽衣伝説関連史跡|date=2019年7月|cat=京丹後市}} 『丹後国風土記』逸文（「比治真奈井　奈具社」という項目）に記述がある。各地に伝わる[[羽衣伝説]]とは違い、物語の筋が一風変わったものになっている。 逸文によれば、丹後国[[中郡 (京都府)|丹波郡]]の比治山（[[磯砂山]]）の山頂にあった{{読み仮名|麻奈井/真名井|まない}}{{efn2|「眞名井」、「眞井」とも。}}の井（泉{{efn2|原文では、物語筆者の当時、既に沼となっていたと書かれる。}}）で、天女八人が水浴びしていたとき、{{読み仮名|和奈佐|わなさ}}という老夫婦が、一人の天女の衣装（[[羽衣 (衣装)|羽衣]]{{Refn|原文では「衣裳」としか伝えないが、羽衣とみなされる。}}）を隠し、恥ずかしがっていた彼女が求めてもなお欺罔を疑って返さず、帰れなくなった天女{{efn2|天女の言い分では、いったん地上にとどまってしまった以上、天界には帰れなくなってしまった、よってどのみち従うしかないと訴えた。}}を養女にした。 天女はたった一杯で万病に効くという酒をつくり、老夫婦と十余年共に暮らした。天女の造る霊酒によって家は豊かに{{読み仮名|土形|ひじかた}}富み、土形の里と呼ばれた。比治の里は土形の里の転訛である。その後、老夫は「汝は吾が子ではない」と言い天女を追い出してしまう。老夫婦の残酷な仕打ちを恨んだ天女が自分の心情を「荒塩」のようだと言い残したので、比治の里は荒塩の村とも呼ばれた。天女は慟哭しつつ比治の里を去り、次の歌を詠む： ::天の原　降り放け見れば　霞立ち　家路惑ひて　行方知らずも 天女が[[ケヤキ|槻の木]]に寄りかかって哭いた場所は{{読み仮名|哭木|なきき}}の村と呼ばれた。 [[竹野郡 (京都府)|竹野郡]]の船木の里に入った天女は「これで私の心は{{読み仮名|奈具志久|なぐしく}}（おだやかに）なった」と言い、天女が留まった村は奈具村と呼ばれた。奈具村には[[奈具神社]]が創建され、祀られたこの天女こそ[[トヨウケビメ|豊宇賀能売命]]（とようかのめのみこと）である、とする{{harvp|校注日本文学大系|1928}}, {{URL|1=https://books.google.com/books?id=4JKEjo-QyyoC&pg=PP207 |2=pp. 189–190}} {{blockquote|丹後國風土記曰。丹後ノ國{{ruby|丹波|たには}}郡ノ郡郡家ノ西北ノ隅方有二{{ruby|比治|ひぢ}}里一、此里比治山頂有レ井、其名曰二{{ruby|眞井|まなゐ}}ト一。今旣ニ成二沼ト。此井ハ天女八人降來ハ浴レ水。于レ時有リ二老夫婦一、。其名ヲ曰二{{ruby|和奈佐老夫|わなさおきな}}、{{ruby|和奈佐老婦|わなさおうな}}ト。此老等至二此井一、而竊取リ二藏シキ天女一人ノ衣裳ヲ一。　卽チ有二衣裳一者皆天飛上リス。　{{ruby|但|ただ}}無二衣裳一女娘一人留リテ、卽身ヲ隱シテ水ニ而獨リ{{ruby|懐愧|はぢを}}居リキ。爰老夫謂二天女一曰、「吾請二天女娘一汝為レ兒。」天女答曰、「妾獨留二レリ人間ニ一、{{ruby|何敢|いかでか}}不ランレ從ハ、請フラクハ{{ruby|許|たま}}ヘトイウ二衣裳ヲ一」。老夫曰フニ三「天女娘　{{ruby|何存|などおも}}フト二欺ク心ノ一」。天女曰ラク「凡天人此ノ志、以レ信為ルニレ本、何デ多ク二疑心一不ルレ許サ二衣裳ヲ一。」老夫答テ曰、「多クシテレ疑無キワレ信、{{ruby|率士之|このくにの}}常ナリ。{{ruby|故|か}}レ以テ二此ノ心ヲ一{{ruby|為|おも}}ヘルレ不レ許耳。遂許シテ卽相副テ而往テレ宅ニ、卽相住メルコト十餘歳。爰ニ天女{{ruby|善爲醸酒|よきさけをかめり}}。飮メバ二{{ruby|一杯|ひとつき}}ヲ一{{ruby|吉|よく}}{{ruby|方病|よろづのやまひ}}ヲ悉ク{{ruby|除之|いやしぬ}}。其一杯之{{ruby|直財|あたひ}}積ミテレ車ニ送リキレ之。于レ時其家豐ニ{{ruby|土形|ひぢかた}}富メリ。故ニ云二土形ノ里一。此自二中閒一至二于今時一便云二比治里一。後老夫婦等謂二天女ニ一曰　「汝ハ非ズ二吾兒一。暫ク借リニ住タル耳。宣二早出去ル一。」{{ruby|於|ここに}}是レ天女仰ギテレ天ヲ{{ruby|哭慟|なげ}}キ。俯シテ地ニ{{ruby|哀吟|かなし}}ミ。卽謂ヒテ二老夫一、等曰「妾非ズ下以テ二吾意一來ルニ上。老夫等ノ所レ願。何モ發シテ二厭惡之心ヲ一。忽ニ存スヤトイフ二出デ去ランムル之痛ヲ一。」老夫增發シレ瞋リテ願レ去。天女流レ淚。徴ク退テ二門外ニ一。謂ニ郷人ニ一曰ク「久シク沈ミ二人間ニ一。不レ得レ還ルコトヲレ天ニ。無ケレバ二{{ruby|親故|したしきもの}}一。{{ruby|不知由所居|をるべきよしをしらず}}。{{ruby|吾|あれ}}{{ruby|何哉何哉|いかにせん〳〵}}トイフ。」拭レ{{ruby|涙嗟歎|なげきつつ}}、仰ギテレ天ヲ歌ヒ曰ケラク：
　 {{ruby|阿痲能波良|あまのはら}}/{{ruby|布理佐兼美禮婆|ふりさけみれば}}/
　 {{ruby|加須美多智|かすみたち}}/{{ruby|伊幣治痲土比天|いへぢまどひて}}/ {{ruby|由久幣志良受母|ゆくへしらずも}}。
　 遂ニ退去リテ而至ル二荒鹽ノ村ニ一。卽謂テ二村人等ニ一云二「思二老夫老婦意一。我心無レ異ナル二荒鹽ノ村ニ一者。」仍テ云二比治ノ里ヲ荒鹽ノ村ト一。亦至リ二丹波ノ里{{ruby|哭木|なきき}}ノ村一。摅リテ二槻木ニ一哭ケリ。故ニ云フ哭木村。復至リ二{{ruby|竹野|たかぬ}}ノ郡船木ノ里{{ruby|奈具|なぐ}}ノ村一。卽謂テ二村人等一云ク「此處我心成リヌ二{{ruby|奈具志久|なぐしく}}一。」（古事ニ平善者云二奈具志一）乃チ留二居此村ニ一。斯ハ所レ謂竹野ノ郡奈具ノ社坐ス{{ruby|豐宇賀能賣|とようかのめ}}ノ命也。}}。 比治の里は現在の[[京丹後市]][[峰山町]]鱒留・峰山町久次に比定されている。鱒留は[[延宝]]年間頃まで益富と表記され、久次にある[[比沼麻奈為神社]]は[[式内社]]の比治真名井神社に比定されている。『丹哥府志』や『丹後旧事記』は現在の京丹後市峰山町荒山を荒塩の村の遺名としている。哭木の村は現在の京丹後市峰山町内記に比定され、名木神社がある。奈具村は[[嘉吉]]年間の洪水で流失していまい、その故地は不明だが、現在の京丹後市[[弥栄町]]黒部に奈具遺跡、同市弥栄町溝谷に奈具岡遺跡という弥生中期の遺跡がある。奈具神社は現在の京丹後市弥栄町船木に再建されている{{sfnp|近江|1993|p=90}}。 == 脚注 == ===注釈=== {{notelist2}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===出典=== {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|和書|ref={{SfnRef|加藤|1902}}|author=加藤咄堂 |author-link=加藤咄堂 |title=日本宗敎風俗志 |publisher=森江書店 |date=1902 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 |pages=374–375 }} {{cite book|和書|ref={{SfnRef|近江|1993}}|author=近江雅和 |author-link= |title=日『記紀』解体 |publisher=彩流社 |date=1993 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 |pages=88–89 |isbn=9784779156236}} {{cite book|和書|ref={{SfnRef|尾崎|1986}} |author=尾崎暢殃 |author-link= |chapter=竹取の翁 |title=国文学年次別論文集: 上代 第2部 |publisher=朋文出版 |date=1986 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VEIzAAAAMAAJ&q=竹取 |pages=242–243}} {{cite book|和書|ref={{SfnRef|谷川|2010 }}|author=谷川健一 |author-link谷川健一 |title=列島縦断地名逍遥 |location= |publisher=冨山房インターナショナル |date=2010 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=X6ADZTG-d6oC&pg=PA205 |p=205 |isbn=9784902385915}} }} ==参照文献== {{refbegin}} * {{citation|和書|ref={{SfnRef|校注日本文学大系|1928}} |chapter=国文学研究資料：(116)羽衣の基礎となれる伝説に就いて |title=校注日本文学大系 |volume=25 |publisher=国民図書|year=1928|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4JKEjo-QyyoC&pg=PP207 |pages=189–190}} {{refend}} == 外部リンク == *『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 風土記]』p.296 「丹後の國」 [[武田祐吉]] 編 岩波書店（国立国会図書館） == 関連項目 == *[[羽衣 (能)]] - [[羽衣伝説]]を題材とした[[能]]の作品。 {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:たんこのくにふとき}} [[Category:丹後国]] [[Category:京都府の歴史]] [[Category:風土記]] [[Category:逸書]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[丹後国風土記]] to [[Tango no Kuni Fudoki]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2008年10月}} '''『丹後国風土記』'''（たんごのくにふどき）は、[[丹後国]]（現在の[[京都府]][[京都府北部地域|北部]]）の[[風土記]]。[[逸文|逸書]]であるため、内容は『[[釈日本紀|釋日本紀]]』などでの引用によるしかない。風土記編纂が命じられたのが[[和銅]]6年（[[713年]]）であるため、原本は遅くとも[[8世紀]]中にはできていたと思われる。 数多くある風土記逸文の中でも比較的長文が残されており、最古の部類に入る[[浦島伝説]]、[[羽衣伝説]]の記述は[[万葉仮名]]書きの[[和歌]]が入っている点も含めて特筆すべきものである。他に、[[天橋立]]の伝承もある（下に挙げる和歌は[[仮名交じり文|漢字仮名交じり文]]で記す）。 また、これとは別に『丹後国風土記残缺』が風土記の写本の一部として伝えられているが、これについては後世の偽書である可能性が高い福岡猛志「『丹後国風土記残欠』の基礎的検討」『愛知県史研究』17号、2013年。 == 浦島伝説 == {{画像提供依頼|浦嶋子伝説関連史跡|date=2019年7月|cat=京都府}} 「筒川嶼子　水江浦島子」という項目に記述がある。「天上仙家」や「蓬山」が出てくるなど中国渡来の[[神仙思想]]が窺える。「水江浦島子」が[[童話]]に出てくる[[浦島太郎]]であるが、彼は[[日下部氏|日下部首]]の一人である。 : 筒川の里、日下部首等の先祖に姿容秀美の筒川嶼子という者、即ち水江浦島子がいた。伊豫部馬養連の記したところのものを述べる。 と前置きした上で、長谷朝倉宮御宇天皇（[[雄略天皇]]）の御世、浦島子は小舟に乗り釣りに出た。三日三晩の間一匹の魚も釣れなかったが五色の亀だけ得る。奇異に思ったが眠っている間に亀は比べることもなき美麗な婦人と為った。女娘は問答の中「天上仙家之人也」と己を語る。彼女が眠るように命じ浦島子が目覚めると、不意の間に海中の大きな島に至っていた。館の門に入ると七人の童子、八人の童子が迎えるが彼らはそれぞれ「すばるぼし」（[[プレアデス星団|プレアデス]]）と「あめふりぼし」（[[ヒアデス星団|ヒヤデス]]）だという。女娘は父母と共に迎え、歓待の合間に人界と仙都の別を説く。館に留まること三年経ち、浦島子は郷里の事を思い出し、神仙之堺に居るよりも俗世に還ることを希望する。女娘は別れを悲しみながらも、玉匣（たまくしげ）を渡し「戻ってくる気ならゆめゆめ開けるなかれ」と忠告する。帰り着いて辺りが変わっているので郷の者に聞くと、浦島子は蒼海に出たまま帰らなかったということにされていた。玉匣を開くと風雲に翩飛けるような変化が起き、浦島子は涙に咽（むせ）び徘徊し、歌を詠む…… 別の書『[[古事談]]』では、「[[淳和天皇]]御宇天長二年（825年）乙巳。丹後国与佐郡人水江浦島子。此年乗松船。到故郷」と記され、そのことから帰還まで350年程度経ったと推定される。出発時の雄略天皇の代がいつなのか確定しがたいが、他の浦島伝説での共通点も踏まえ現世では館での3年より遙かに長い時間が流れていたと伝えられることは確実なようである。 == 羽衣伝説 == {{also|奈具神社#由緒}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|磯砂山の登山口付近の「羽衣茶屋」]] {{画像提供依頼|羽衣伝説関連史跡|date=2019年7月|cat=京丹後市}} 『丹後国風土記』逸文（「比治真奈井　奈具社」という項目）に記述がある。各地に伝わる[[羽衣伝説]]とは違い、物語の筋が一風変わったものになっている。 逸文によれば、丹後国[[中郡 (京都府)|丹波郡]]の比治山（[[磯砂山]]）の山頂にあった{{読み仮名|麻奈井/真名井|まない}}{{efn2|「眞名井」、「眞井」とも。}}の井（泉{{efn2|原文では、物語筆者の当時、既に沼となっていたと書かれる。}}）で、天女八人が水浴びしていたとき、{{読み仮名|和奈佐|わなさ}}という老夫婦が、一人の天女の衣装（[[羽衣 (衣装)|羽衣]]{{Refn|原文では「衣裳」としか伝えないが、羽衣とみなされる。}}）を隠し、恥ずかしがっていた彼女が求めてもなお欺罔を疑って返さず、帰れなくなった天女{{efn2|天女の言い分では、いったん地上にとどまってしまった以上、天界には帰れなくなってしまった、よってどのみち従うしかないと訴えた。}}を養女にした。 天女はたった一杯で万病に効くという酒をつくり、老夫婦と十余年共に暮らした。天女の造る霊酒によって家は豊かに{{読み仮名|土形|ひじかた}}富み、土形の里と呼ばれた。比治の里は土形の里の転訛である。その後、老夫は「汝は吾が子ではない」と言い天女を追い出してしまう。老夫婦の残酷な仕打ちを恨んだ天女が自分の心情を「荒塩」のようだと言い残したので、比治の里は荒塩の村とも呼ばれた。天女は慟哭しつつ比治の里を去り、次の歌を詠む： ::天の原　降り放け見れば　霞立ち　家路惑ひて　行方知らずも 天女が[[ケヤキ|槻の木]]に寄りかかって哭いた場所は{{読み仮名|哭木|なきき}}の村と呼ばれた。 [[竹野郡 (京都府)|竹野郡]]の船木の里に入った天女は「これで私の心は{{読み仮名|奈具志久|なぐしく}}（おだやかに）なった」と言い、天女が留まった村は奈具村と呼ばれた。奈具村には[[奈具神社]]が創建され、祀られたこの天女こそ[[トヨウケビメ|豊宇賀能売命]]（とようかのめのみこと）である、とする{{harvp|校注日本文学大系|1928}}, {{URL|1=https://books.google.com/books?id=4JKEjo-QyyoC&pg=PP207 |2=pp. 189–190}} {{blockquote|丹後國風土記曰。丹後ノ國{{ruby|丹波|たには}}郡ノ郡郡家ノ西北ノ隅方有二{{ruby|比治|ひぢ}}里一、此里比治山頂有レ井、其名曰二{{ruby|眞井|まなゐ}}ト一。今旣ニ成二沼ト。此井ハ天女八人降來ハ浴レ水。于レ時有リ二老夫婦一、。其名ヲ曰二{{ruby|和奈佐老夫|わなさおきな}}、{{ruby|和奈佐老婦|わなさおうな}}ト。此老等至二此井一、而竊取リ二藏シキ天女一人ノ衣裳ヲ一。　卽チ有二衣裳一者皆天飛上リス。　{{ruby|但|ただ}}無二衣裳一女娘一人留リテ、卽身ヲ隱シテ水ニ而獨リ{{ruby|懐愧|はぢを}}居リキ。爰老夫謂二天女一曰、「吾請二天女娘一汝為レ兒。」天女答曰、「妾獨留二レリ人間ニ一、{{ruby|何敢|いかでか}}不ランレ從ハ、請フラクハ{{ruby|許|たま}}ヘトイウ二衣裳ヲ一」。老夫曰フニ三「天女娘　{{ruby|何存|などおも}}フト二欺ク心ノ一」。天女曰ラク「凡天人此ノ志、以レ信為ルニレ本、何デ多ク二疑心一不ルレ許サ二衣裳ヲ一。」老夫答テ曰、「多クシテレ疑無キワレ信、{{ruby|率士之|このくにの}}常ナリ。{{ruby|故|か}}レ以テ二此ノ心ヲ一{{ruby|為|おも}}ヘルレ不レ許耳。遂許シテ卽相副テ而往テレ宅ニ、卽相住メルコト十餘歳。爰ニ天女{{ruby|善爲醸酒|よきさけをかめり}}。飮メバ二{{ruby|一杯|ひとつき}}ヲ一{{ruby|吉|よく}}{{ruby|方病|よろづのやまひ}}ヲ悉ク{{ruby|除之|いやしぬ}}。其一杯之{{ruby|直財|あたひ}}積ミテレ車ニ送リキレ之。于レ時其家豐ニ{{ruby|土形|ひぢかた}}富メリ。故ニ云二土形ノ里一。此自二中閒一至二于今時一便云二比治里一。後老夫婦等謂二天女ニ一曰　「汝ハ非ズ二吾兒一。暫ク借リニ住タル耳。宣二早出去ル一。」{{ruby|於|ここに}}是レ天女仰ギテレ天ヲ{{ruby|哭慟|なげ}}キ。俯シテ地ニ{{ruby|哀吟|かなし}}ミ。卽謂ヒテ二老夫一、等曰「妾非ズ下以テ二吾意一來ルニ上。老夫等ノ所レ願。何モ發シテ二厭惡之心ヲ一。忽ニ存スヤトイフ二出デ去ランムル之痛ヲ一。」老夫增發シレ瞋リテ願レ去。天女流レ淚。徴ク退テ二門外ニ一。謂ニ郷人ニ一曰ク「久シク沈ミ二人間ニ一。不レ得レ還ルコトヲレ天ニ。無ケレバ二{{ruby|親故|したしきもの}}一。{{ruby|不知由所居|をるべきよしをしらず}}。{{ruby|吾|あれ}}{{ruby|何哉何哉|いかにせん〳〵}}トイフ。」拭レ{{ruby|涙嗟歎|なげきつつ}}、仰ギテレ天ヲ歌ヒ曰ケラク：
　 {{ruby|阿痲能波良|あまのはら}}/{{ruby|布理佐兼美禮婆|ふりさけみれば}}/
　 {{ruby|加須美多智|かすみたち}}/{{ruby|伊幣治痲土比天|いへぢまどひて}}/ {{ruby|由久幣志良受母|ゆくへしらずも}}。
　 遂ニ退去リテ而至ル二荒鹽ノ村ニ一。卽謂テ二村人等ニ一云二「思二老夫老婦意一。我心無レ異ナル二荒鹽ノ村ニ一者。」仍テ云二比治ノ里ヲ荒鹽ノ村ト一。亦至リ二丹波ノ里{{ruby|哭木|なきき}}ノ村一。摅リテ二槻木ニ一哭ケリ。故ニ云フ哭木村。復至リ二{{ruby|竹野|たかぬ}}ノ郡船木ノ里{{ruby|奈具|なぐ}}ノ村一。卽謂テ二村人等一云ク「此處我心成リヌ二{{ruby|奈具志久|なぐしく}}一。」（古事ニ平善者云二奈具志一）乃チ留二居此村ニ一。斯ハ所レ謂竹野ノ郡奈具ノ社坐ス{{ruby|豐宇賀能賣|とようかのめ}}ノ命也。}}。 比治の里は現在の[[京丹後市]][[峰山町]]鱒留・峰山町久次に比定されている。鱒留は[[延宝]]年間頃まで益富と表記され、久次にある[[比沼麻奈為神社]]は[[式内社]]の比治真名井神社に比定されている。『丹哥府志』や『丹後旧事記』は現在の京丹後市峰山町荒山を荒塩の村の遺名としている。哭木の村は現在の京丹後市峰山町内記に比定され、名木神社がある。奈具村は[[嘉吉]]年間の洪水で流失していまい、その故地は不明だが、現在の京丹後市[[弥栄町]]黒部に奈具遺跡、同市弥栄町溝谷に奈具岡遺跡という弥生中期の遺跡がある。奈具神社は現在の京丹後市弥栄町船木に再建されている{{sfnp|近江|1993|p=90}}。 == 脚注 == ===注釈=== {{notelist2}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} ===出典=== {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book|和書|ref={{SfnRef|加藤|1902}}|author=加藤咄堂 |author-link=加藤咄堂 |title=日本宗敎風俗志 |publisher=森江書店 |date=1902 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 |pages=374–375 }} {{cite book|和書|ref={{SfnRef|近江|1993}}|author=近江雅和 |author-link= |title=日『記紀』解体 |publisher=彩流社 |date=1993 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 |pages=88–89 |isbn=9784779156236}} {{cite book|和書|ref={{SfnRef|尾崎|1986}} |author=尾崎暢殃 |author-link= |chapter=竹取の翁 |title=国文学年次別論文集: 上代 第2部 |publisher=朋文出版 |date=1986 |chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VEIzAAAAMAAJ&q=竹取 |pages=242–243}} {{cite book|和書|ref={{SfnRef|谷川|2010 }}|author=谷川健一 |author-link谷川健一 |title=列島縦断地名逍遥 |location= |publisher=冨山房インターナショナル |date=2010 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=X6ADZTG-d6oC&pg=PA205 |p=205 |isbn=9784902385915}} }} ==参照文献== {{refbegin}} * {{citation|和書|ref={{SfnRef|校注日本文学大系|1928}} |chapter=国文学研究資料：(116)羽衣の基礎となれる伝説に就いて |title=校注日本文学大系 |volume=25 |publisher=国民図書|year=1928|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4JKEjo-QyyoC&pg=PP207 |pages=189–190}} {{refend}} == 外部リンク == *『[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 風土記]』p.296 「丹後の國」 [[武田祐吉]] 編 岩波書店（国立国会図書館） == 関連項目 == *[[羽衣 (能)]] - [[羽衣伝説]]を題材とした[[能]]の作品。 {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:たんこのくにふとき}} [[Category:丹後国]] [[Category:京都府の歴史]] [[Category:風土記]] [[Category:逸書]]

{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (present-day northern {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). As a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸文|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hagoromo Legend|ja|羽衣伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These excerpts feature waka written in [[man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the {{ill|Amanohashidate|ja|天橋立|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ({{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in modern-day {{ill|Kyotango City|ja|京丹後市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.

{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Tango Province|ja|丹後国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (present-day northern {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|ja|京都府|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). As a {{ill|lost work|ja|逸文|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hagoromo Legend|ja|羽衣伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These excerpts feature waka written in [[man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the {{ill|Amanohashidate|ja|天橋立|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ({{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in modern-day {{ill|Kyotango City|ja|京丹後市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by {{ill|Takeda Sukeyoshi|ja|武田祐吉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hagoromo (Noh)|ja|羽衣|qq=draft|qq=draft}} - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=}}, in modern-day {{ill|Kyotango City|ja|京丹後市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyotango City|qq=}}). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|ja|丹波国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanba Province|qq=}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=}}.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|qq=}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|qq=}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by [[:en:Yūkichi Takeda|Takeda Sukeyoshi]], Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Hagoromo|Hagoromo (Noh)]] - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.

{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|qq=}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|qq=}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by [[:en:Yūkichi Takeda|Takeda Sukeyoshi]], Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Hagoromo|Hagoromo (Noh)]] - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=}}.

<!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--> [[Category:京丹後市の画像提供依頼]] [[Category:Wikipedia requested photographs in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:風土記]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:丹後國風土記]]

{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|qq=}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|qq=}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by [[:en:Yūkichi Takeda|Takeda Sukeyoshi]], Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Hagoromo|Hagoromo (Noh)]] - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.

Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>

== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

revision.text:
{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by [[:en:Yūkichi Takeda|Takeda Sukeyoshi]], Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Hagoromo|Hagoromo (Noh)]] - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.

[[sn:Tango no Kuni Fudoki]] [[ja:丹後国風土記]]
<!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--> [[Category:Image requests in Kyotango City]] [[Category:Wikipedia requested photographs in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:丹後國風土記]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11368517]] Wikidata name: Tango no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by [[:en:Yūkichi Takeda|Takeda Sukeyoshi]], Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Hagoromo|Hagoromo (Noh)]] - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.
