<!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--> [[Category:Image requests in Kyotango City]] [[Category:Wikipedia requested photographs in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11368517}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11368517]] Wikidata name: Tango no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|qq=|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|qq=|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}

Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

<!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--> [[Category:Image requests in Kyotango City]] [[Category:Wikipedia requested photographs in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Fudoki]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11368517}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11368517]] Wikidata name: Tango no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.

The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11368517]] Wikidata name: Tango no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}

Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

revision.comment: Bot: reorganize categories (alphabetical, deduplicated, moved to bottom)
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11368517]] Wikidata name: Tango no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by [[:en:Yūkichi Takeda|Takeda Sukeyoshi]], Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Hagoromo|Hagoromo (Noh)]] - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.

<!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11368517}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:History of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Image requests in Kyotango City]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Tango Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikipedia requested photographs in Kyoto Prefecture]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11368517]] Wikidata name: Tango no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of [[:en:Tango Province|Tango Province]] (present-day northern [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]). As a [[:en:Lost literary work|lost work]], its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''[[:en:Shaku Nihongi|Shaku Nihongi]]''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and [[:en:Swan maiden|Hagoromo Legend]]. These excerpts feature waka written in [[:en:Man'yōgana|Man'yōgana]], a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the [[:en:Amanohashidate|Amanohashidate]] (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ([[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]]), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day [[:en:Kyōtango|Kyotango City]]). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in [[:en:Tanba Province|Tanba Province]], where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by [[:en:Yūkichi Takeda|Takeda Sukeyoshi]], Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Hagoromo|Hagoromo (Noh)]] - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11368517]] Wikidata name: Tango no Kuni Fudoki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:50, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|WD=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province}} (present-day northern {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}). As a {{ill|Lost literary work|en|Lost literary work|WD=Q1585442|lt=lost work}}, its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''{{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihongi}}''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and {{ill|Swan maiden|en|Swan maiden|WD=Q1326754|lt=Hagoromo Legend}}. These excerpts feature waka written in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana}}, a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the {{ill|Amanohashidate|en|Amanohashidate|WD=Q259727|lt=Amanohashidate}} (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ({{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day {{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyotango City}}). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by {{ill|Yūkichi Takeda|en|Yūkichi Takeda|WD=Q2165204|lt=Takeda Sukeyoshi}}, Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hagoromo|en|Hagoromo|WD=Q1208551|lt=Hagoromo (Noh)}} - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|丹後国風土記|version=103401231|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]]
{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|WD=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province}} (present-day northern {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}). As a {{ill|Lost literary work|en|Lost literary work|WD=Q1585442|lt=lost work}}, its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''{{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihongi}}''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and {{ill|Swan maiden|en|Swan maiden|WD=Q1326754|lt=Hagoromo Legend}}. These excerpts feature waka written in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana}}, a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the {{ill|Amanohashidate|en|Amanohashidate|WD=Q259727|lt=Amanohashidate}} (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=immortal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ({{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day {{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyotango City}}). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
== Notes == {{notelist2}} {{Citation help}}
== References == {{Reflist|refs= <ref name="kato1902">{{Cite book|和書|author=Kato Totsudo |title=Japanese Religious Customs and Traditions |publisher=Moroe Shoten |date=1902 |pages=374–375 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xP0kAAAAMAAJ&pg=PA374 }}</ref>
<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}
== Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Annotated Anthology of Japanese Literature |publisher=Kokumin Tosho |year=1928 |pages=189–190}}
== External Links == * ''[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1173165/152 Fudoki],'' p.296: "Tango Province," edited by {{ill|Yūkichi Takeda|en|Yūkichi Takeda|WD=Q2165204|lt=Takeda Sukeyoshi}}, Iwanami Shoten (National Diet Library).
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hagoromo|en|Hagoromo|WD=Q1208551|lt=Hagoromo (Noh)}} - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--><!--Tango no Kuni Fudoki--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[zh:丹後國風土記]] {{wikidata link|Q11368517}} [[Category:Fudoki]] [[Category:History of Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Image requests in Kyotango City]] [[Category:Lost books]] [[Category:Lost works]] [[Category:Tango Province]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikipedia requested photographs in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:丹後国風土記]][[zh:丹後國風土記]]
{{Citation needed|date=October 2008}} '''Tango no Kuni Fudoki''' (丹後国風土記) is the [[Fudoki]] of {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|WD=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province|1=Tango Province}} (present-day northern {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}). As a {{ill|Lost literary work|en|Lost literary work|WD=Q1585442|lt=lost work|1=Lost literary work}}, its content can only be known through citations in texts such as the ''{{ill|Shaku Nihongi|en|Shaku Nihongi|WD=Q2710483|lt=Shaku Nihongi|1=Shaku Nihongi}}''. Since the compilation of Fudoki was ordered in [[:en:Wadō|Wadō]] 6 (713), the original text is believed to have been completed no later than the 8th century.
Among the numerous Fudoki fragments, the ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki'' stands out for its relatively long surviving excerpts, including some of the earliest records of the {{ill|Urashima Tarō|lt=Urashima Tarō Legend|ja|浦島伝説|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Urashima Tarō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 浦島太郎}} and {{ill|Swan maiden|en|Swan maiden|WD=Q1326754|lt=Hagoromo Legend|1=Swan maiden}}. These excerpts feature waka written in {{ill|Man'yōgana|en|Man'yōgana|WD=Q862803|lt=Man'yōgana|1=Man'yōgana}}, a noteworthy characteristic. Additionally, the text includes traditions about the {{ill|Amanohashidate|en|Amanohashidate|WD=Q259727|lt=Amanohashidate|1=Amanohashidate}} (Bridge to Heaven).
Separately, a text titled ''Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments'' is transmitted as part of a Fudoki manuscript. However, this text is likely a later forgery.<ref>Fukuoka Takashi, "A Basic Examination of the 'Tango no Kuni Fudoki Fragments,'" ''Aichi Historical Research,'' Issue 17, 2013.</ref>
== Urashima Legend == {{Image request|Urashimako legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyoto Prefecture}} A section titled ''Tsutsugawa Islander, Urashimako of Mizue'' describes the story of Urashimako, tracing elements of {{ill|Xian (Taoism)|lt=Chinese immortal thought|ja|神仙思想|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xian (Taoism)|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仙人|1=Xian (Taoism)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1207451}} such as "heavenly immortal family" and "Hōzan" (Penglai). Urashimako, identified as the Urashima Tarō of later folklore, is described as a member of the {{ill|Kusakabe clan|ja|日下部氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kusakabe clan|qq=}}.
:In the village of Tsutsugawa, a man named Tsutsugawa no Shiko, ancestor of the Kusakabe clan, was known for his extraordinary beauty. This was Urashimako of Mizue. According to records by Iyobe Umayoshi-no-Muraji, during the reign of Emperor Hase-Asakura ({{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}), Urashimako went fishing in a small boat. For three days and nights, he caught no fish but captured a five-colored turtle. He fell asleep, and the turtle transformed into an incomparably beautiful woman, who revealed herself to be a "person of the heavenly immortal family." She directed him to sleep again, and when he awoke, he found himself on a grand island. Within a magnificent palace, seven and eight boys greeted him as the Pleiades and Hyades constellations.
After living there for three years, Urashimako remembered his hometown and decided to return, despite warnings. He was given a jeweled box (''tamakushige'') and instructed never to open it. Upon returning, he found his village utterly changed and learned that he had been declared lost at sea. In despair, he opened the box, unleashing wind and clouds, and composed the following waka:
::At the shore of eternity, clouds drift and scatter; ::Urashimako of Mizue carries words across the waves.
Later, others composed:
::Had Urashimako of Mizue never opened the jeweled box, ::We would have met again.
The legend parallels accounts in the {{ill|Konjaku Monogatari|ja|今昔物語|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konjaku Monogatari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 今昔物語集|1=Konjaku Monogatarishū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1507085}} and ''Kojidan,'' which estimate a 350-year interval between his departure and return.
== Hagoromo Legend == {{also|Nagu Shrine#History}} [[File:Hagoromotyaya.jpg|thumb|The "Hagoromo Teahouse" near the base of {{ill|Isosaki Mountain|ja|磯砂山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Isosaki Mountain|1=Mount Isanago|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q31707112}} {{Image request|Hagoromo legend-related historical sites|date=July 2019|cat=Kyotango City}} The Hagoromo Legend, preserved as a fragment under the heading "Hijimana Nagu Shrine," presents a unique variation on the celestial maiden story compared to other regions.
According to the fragment, celestial maidens bathed in a spring called ''Manai'' (located atop {{ill|Hijiyama|ja|比治山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hijiyama|1=Mount Hiji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11126130}}, in modern-day {{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|WD=Q513897|lt=Kyotango City|1=Kyōtango}}). An elderly couple, {{ill|Wanasa|ja|和奈佐|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wanasa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, stole the robe of one maiden, preventing her from returning to the heavens. The maiden became their adopted daughter and created miraculous sake that healed all illnesses. However, after ten years, they grew wealthy and cast her out. The maiden lamented, composing the following:
::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

<ref name="oumi1993">{{Cite book|和書|author=Oumi Masakazu |title=Deconstructing the Chronicles |publisher=Sairyusha |date=1993 |pages=88–89 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=uFKwDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA88 }}</ref> }}

== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hagoromo|en|Hagoromo|WD=Q1208551|lt=Hagoromo (Noh)|1=Hagoromo}} - A Noh play based on the Hagoromo Legend.

::Looking down upon the vast sky, ::Mist rises as I wander, ::Lost and far from my home.
The once-flourishing village declined and became known as the "Village of Harsh Salt" (''Arashio'').
The maiden traveled to the village of Nakiki in {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}, where she stated her heart had "become calm" (''nagushiku''). Here, {{ill|Nagu Shrine|ja|奈具神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagu Shrine|qq=}} was established, enshrining her as {{ill|Toyouke-bime|ja|豊宇賀能売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-bime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
